metadata.name: W4M-WWG AU-UK Chilled/Frozen Meat Corridor — Instance Data
metadata.domain: International Meat Trade / Cold Chain Logistics / Wholesale Distribution
metadata.description: Complete graph of the W4M-WWG (World Wide Grocers) Australia-UK chilled/frozen meat corridor. Toowoomba abattoir through Brisbane, Singapore transhipment, Suez transit, Felixstowe port, BCP inspection, Tilbury alternative, Daventry WWG cold store, distribution hub, and customer delivery. Includes 11 chain nodes, 9 shipment legs, 12 parties, 8 compliance gates, 14 trade documents, 9 bottlenecks with risk and impact assessments, 5 hypotheses, 4 scenarios, 6 cascade effects, 4 predictive patterns, 3 incident examples with escalation chains, and 1 example shipment.

examples.name: WWG Queensland Grass-Fed Beef - UK Wholesale Distribution
examples.description: Chilled grass-fed beef from DAFF-accredited abattoir in Toowoomba QLD to WWG wholesale distribution network via Port of Brisbane and Felixstowe. WWG acts as importer of record and wholesale distributor to UK food service, hospitality, and independent retail. 90-day shelf life from slaughter, ~38 day transit including BCP, leaving 49-52 days for UK wholesale distribution. AU-UK FTA preferential tariff (0% on beef quota). CIF Felixstowe terms. Tilbury maintained as alternative discharge port.
examples.regulatoryRegime: AU-UK FTA 2023; AU Export Control Act 2020; UK BTOM 2024

examples.name: Toowoomba Abattoir & Processing (Origin)
examples.capabilities: slaughter, boning, vacuum-pack, blast-chill, DAFF-accredited, halal-certified, MSA-graded, grass-fed traceability (NLIS)

examples.name: Brisbane Cold Storage (Container Loading & Staging)
examples.capabilities: 0-2C holding, container pre-cool verification, reefer pre-trip inspection staging, palletisation, export documentation staging, weight verification

examples.name: Port of Brisbane - Reefer Terminal
examples.capabilities: reefer-plug, container-yard, customs-clearance, DAFF-inspection, weighbridge, VGM certification

examples.name: Singapore Transhipment Hub
examples.capabilities: reefer-plug, transhipment-handling, customs-transit, container-inspection, 24/7 reefer monitoring

examples.name: Suez Canal Transit Waypoint
examples.location: Suez Canal, Egypt (Port Said to Suez)

examples.name: Port of Felixstowe - Reefer Terminal
examples.capabilities: reefer-plug, customs-clearance, HMRC-border-force, container-yard, inland-rail-connection, 2400 reefer points

examples.name: Felixstowe Border Control Post (BCP)
examples.operator: APHA (Animal and Plant Health Agency) / Port Health Authority
examples.capabilities: SPS-inspection, document-verification, identity-check, physical-inspection, laboratory-sampling, temperature-data-download

examples.name: Port of Tilbury - Alternative Discharge Port
examples.operator: Forth Ports (Port of Tilbury London Ltd)

examples.name: Daventry WWG Cold Store (Primary Distribution Centre)
examples.location: DIRFT (Daventry International Rail Freight Terminal), Northamptonshire
examples.capabilities: 0-2C holding, multi-customer, pick-and-pack, wholesale-portioning, date-code-management, stock-rotation-FIFO, quality-inspection

examples.name: Customer Delivery Point (Final Destination)
examples.location: Various (food service, hospitality, independent retail across England and Wales)

examples.distance: 12 km (Lytton cold store to Fisherman Islands port)

examples.route: Brisbane then Coral Sea then Torres Strait then Singapore
Singapore then Malacca Strait then Indian Ocean then Red Sea then Suez Canal then Mediterranean then English Channel then Felixstowe
examples.insuranceCoverage: Marine Cargo All-Risk (ICC-A) + War Risk Surcharge, AUD 250,000

examples.estimatedDuration: +1 day vs Felixstowe (vessel calls Tilbury second)
examples.distance: 65 nautical miles (Felixstowe to Tilbury via Thames Estuary)
examples.note: Alternative leg activated when Felixstowe congestion >3 days or BCP capacity exhausted. Container discharged Tilbury, BCP-cleared there, then road to Daventry (195 km, 3.5 hours).

examples.name: Darling Downs Beef Pty Ltd
examples.role: Meat processor and exporter - slaughter, processing, packing, export documentation. Exclusive WWG supplier for grass-fed primals.
examples.responsibilities: Product quality, DAFF compliance, health certificate application, container loading, shelf-life guarantee, NLIS traceability, MSA grading
examples.riskOwnership: Product quality to FOB. Cold chain to FCA Toowoomba. Regulatory compliance (AU side). Shelf-life guarantee 90 days from slaughter.
examples.licences: DAFF Registered Establishment AU-1247, Halal Certification HC-QLD-0891, MSA Licence, NLIS Accredited

examples.name: World Wide Grocers Ltd (WWG)
examples.role: UK importer of record, wholesale distributor, and cold chain owner. WWG holds all UK-side risk and manages distribution to food service, hospitality, and independent retail.
examples.responsibilities: UK import compliance, IPAFFS pre-notification, CHED-P submission, duty payment, cold storage management, wholesale distribution, customer order fulfilment, quality assurance
examples.riskOwnership: All risk from CIF Felixstowe. UK regulatory compliance. Storage, distribution, and customer delivery. Margin ownership.
examples.licences: HMRC EORI GB-714829365000, IPAFFS Registered Importer, DEFRA Approved Food Business Operator, SALSA Accredited

examples.name: SCT Logistics (Freight Forwarding Division)
examples.role: Origin freight forwarder - booking, documentation coordination, container logistics, pre-cool management
examples.responsibilities: Carrier booking, container pre-cool verification, export customs lodgement coordination, B/L instruction, insurance arrangement, cold store liaison
examples.riskOwnership: Operational coordination. No cargo risk (agent only). SLA penalty for missed port cut-off.

examples.name: Hapag-Lloyd AG
examples.role: Ocean carrier - Brisbane to Felixstowe via Singapore transhipment. Primary carrier under 12-month volume contract with WWG.
examples.responsibilities: Vessel operation, reefer container supply, temperature monitoring at sea, on-time arrival, B/L issuance, pre-trip inspection records
examples.riskOwnership: Carrier liability under Hague-Visby Rules (SDR 2 per kg or SDR 666.67 per package). Reefer machinery breakdown. Transhipment handling.

examples.name: Expedient Customs Brokers Pty Ltd
examples.role: Australian customs broker - export declaration, tariff classification, FTA origin certification
examples.responsibilities: Export declaration lodgement (ICS), FTA Certificate of Origin preparation, duty drawback claims, tariff classification
examples.riskOwnership: Declaration accuracy. Penalty liability for misclassification.

examples.name: Clearfreight UK Ltd
examples.role: UK customs broker - import declaration, BTOM compliance, duty/VAT, FTA preference claims on behalf of WWG
examples.responsibilities: Import customs declaration (CDS), tariff classification, FTA preference claim, duty payment, BTOM compliance, simplified declaration procedures
examples.riskOwnership: Declaration accuracy. Duty underpayment liability. Joint responsibility with WWG for FTA claims.

examples.name: DAFF (Department of Agriculture, Fisheries & Forestry)
examples.role: Australian export regulatory authority - meat inspection, health certification, establishment audit
examples.responsibilities: Ante/post-mortem inspection, health certificate issuance, export permit, establishment registration, residue testing programme
examples.riskOwnership: Regulatory authority - sets the rules. Non-negotiable.
examples.contactSLA: Government SLA (2-5 business days standard, 24 hours urgent)

examples.name: APHA (Animal & Plant Health Agency)
examples.role: UK border SPS authority - import inspection, health certificate verification, physical checks at BCP
examples.responsibilities: CHED-P verification, identity checks, physical inspection (random and risk-based), laboratory sampling, import authorisation, temperature log review
examples.riskOwnership: Regulatory authority - can reject, detain, or destroy non-compliant consignments.

examples.name: Toll Refrigerated Transport
examples.role: Domestic reefer haulage - Toowoomba to Brisbane cold store and on to Port of Brisbane
examples.responsibilities: Reefer truck operation, temperature maintenance, GPS tracking, timely delivery to port cut-off, container pre-cool transport
examples.riskOwnership: In-transit temperature. Delay to port. Vehicle breakdown.

examples.name: Reed Boardall Transport Ltd
examples.role: UK inland reefer haulage - Felixstowe BCP to Daventry WWG cold store
examples.responsibilities: Container collection from BCP, reefer temperature maintenance, delivery to WWG cold store, empty container return to depot
examples.riskOwnership: In-transit temperature UK side. Delay to cold store. Demurrage if late collection.

examples.name: Lineage Logistics UK
examples.role: Overflow cold storage partner - activated when WWG Daventry DC at capacity or for Tilbury-routed containers
examples.responsibilities: Temperature-controlled storage overflow, stock rotation (FIFO), date-code management, cross-dock capability, emergency capacity
examples.riskOwnership: Storage temperature integrity. Stock loss through date expiry. Overflow SLA compliance.

examples.name: WWG Distribution Services
examples.role: WWG in-house wholesale distribution arm - Daventry to regional hubs and last-mile customer delivery
examples.responsibilities: Order fulfilment, regional hub management, multi-drop route planning, temperature-controlled delivery, POD (proof of delivery), returns management
examples.riskOwnership: Last-mile temperature. On-time delivery to customer. Stock integrity during distribution.

examples.name: DAFF Health Certificate & Export Permit
examples.commonFailureReasons: Residue test positive (MRL breach), document mismatch between health cert and commercial invoice, establishment audit non-conformance, NLIS traceability gap

examples.name: Australian Export Customs Clearance
examples.commonFailureReasons: Tariff classification dispute, incomplete export declaration, sanctions screening flag

examples.name: IPAFFS Pre-Notification (UK)
examples.avgDuration: Must be submitted 1-5 days before arrival
examples.commonFailureReasons: Late submission, health certificate number mismatch, commodity code error, missing CHED-P reference, WWG EORI mismatch

examples.name: BCP Document & Identity Check
examples.commonFailureReasons: Health cert / CHED-P mismatch, seal number discrepancy, container markings inconsistent with documentation

examples.name: BCP Physical / SPS Inspection
examples.commonFailureReasons: Temperature excursion evidence, visual contamination, off-odour, positive lab result (E.coli O157, Salmonella), packaging damage, incorrect labelling

examples.name: UK Import Customs Clearance (CDS)
examples.commonFailureReasons: Incorrect tariff code, FTA preference claim rejected (origin proof insufficient), duty underpayment, WWG EORI validation failure

examples.name: Cold Chain Temperature Verification
examples.authority: APHA / Port Health (mandatory for all chilled meat)
examples.commonFailureReasons: Temperature excursion >5C recorded, data logger malfunction, gaps in recording, dual logger disagreement

examples.name: AU-UK FTA Preferential Origin Verification
examples.avgDuration: Auto-processed with customs declaration (or 5-10 days if audited)
examples.commonFailureReasons: Insufficient origin documentation, product not wholly obtained (mixed-origin feed lot), declaration of origin wording non-compliant with AU-UK FTA Article 3.16

examples._section: === BOTTLENECKS (The Tight Spots - 9 Bottlenecks) with Risk + Net Impact ===

examples.name: Reefer Container Shortage (Brisbane)
examples.description: Global reefer container imbalance. Empty reefers accumulate in import-heavy regions (Europe, US) while AU faces chronic shortages Oct-Mar. Wait times for 40ft HC reefers extend to 2-3 weeks at peak. WWG volume contract with Hapag mitigates but does not eliminate risk.
examples.rootCause: Structural global imbalance in reefer repositioning; shipping lines prioritise headhaul profitability over empty returns
examples.mitigationStrategy: Hapag volume contract guarantees 6 reefers/month minimum; forward booking 6-8 weeks ahead; multi-carrier fallback (MSC, ONE)
examples.workaroundDescription: Switch to Melbourne or Sydney port if Brisbane reefer availability <80%; accept 20ft reefer at higher per-unit cost; activate MSC as secondary carrier

examples.name: Suez Canal Disruption / Red Sea Re-routing
examples.description: Houthi attacks force vessels via Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 days and AUD 8,000-15,000 per container in fuel/war-risk surcharges. Even when open, canal congestion adds 1-2 days variability. For WWG chilled product, Cape routing pushes transit beyond shelf-life viability threshold.
examples.rootCause: Regional military conflict; shipping lane chokepoint vulnerability
examples.mitigationStrategy: War risk insurance; dual-route pricing in WWG contracts; shelf-life buffer planning for Cape route; frozen fallback protocol
examples.workaroundDescription: Cape of Good Hope routing. Requires shelf-life >55 days at dispatch. WWG activates frozen conversion protocol if Cape probability >30%.

examples.name: BCP Inspection Queue & Lab Turnaround
examples.description: UK BTOM physical inspection rates for AU beef run 15-30%. Lab sample turnaround 2-5 days. BCP has limited reefer holding capacity. Christmas/bank holidays see skeleton staffing. WWG containers queued behind other importers with no priority mechanism.
examples.rootCause: BTOM inspection regime; limited BCP capacity; lab resource constraints
examples.mitigationStrategy: Pre-arrival documentation quality checks by WWG compliance team; avoid Monday/Friday arrivals; maintain consistent supplier quality to build APHA trust profile

examples.name: UK Cold Storage Capacity Shortage
examples.description: UK chilled storage operating at 92-96% capacity nationally. Christmas hits 99%+. WWG operates own Daventry DC but overflow requires Lineage Logistics at premium rates. Energy costs (+40% since 2022) stalling new builds across sector.
examples.typicalDelay: 1-3 days (finding overflow capacity) plus additional transport if redirected
examples.rootCause: Structural underinvestment in UK cold chain infrastructure; energy cost inflation; planning constraints; WWG Daventry DC at 85% base utilisation
examples.mitigationStrategy: WWG 12-month Lineage overflow contract; pre-book 8 weeks for peak; stagger arrivals across Oct-Nov; secondary facility at Grimsby
examples.workaroundDescription: Redirect overflow to Lineage Logistics (DIRFT or Grimsby) at +15% cost; short-term reefer storage at Felixstowe port (max 5 days) at GBP 85/day

examples.name: IPAFFS Pre-Notification Errors
examples.description: IPAFFS system error-prone: commodity code lookup inconsistent, health cert cross-referencing fails silently, strict deadlines. WWG experiencing 8% rejection rate on first submission despite dedicated IPAFFS staff.
examples.rootCause: IPAFFS system UX quality; inconsistent commodity code mapping; manual data entry across 3 systems (IPAFFS, CDS, health cert)
examples.mitigationStrategy: WWG pre-submission validation checklist; dedicated IPAFFS-trained staff (2 FTE); submit 5 days before arrival; cross-reference automation tool in development

examples.name: UK Wholesale Demand Spike (BBQ / Christmas / Events)
examples.description: Demand spikes +25-35% BBQ season, +15-20% Christmas, plus event-driven spikes (bank holiday weekends, major sporting events). WWG wholesale customers place late orders amplifying upstream pressure. 15-30% total chain cost increase during peaks.
examples.typicalDelay: No delay - but cost increase 15-30% across chain
examples.rootCause: Predictable seasonal demand amplified by wholesale customer just-in-time ordering; food service event-driven volatility
examples.mitigationStrategy: WWG forward contracts 3-6 months ahead; pre-position buffer stock at Daventry; negotiate annual freight rates with Hapag; customer deposit scheme for peak orders

examples.name: Port of Felixstowe Congestion
examples.description: UKs largest container port (48% of trade). Periodic congestion from vessel bunching, weather, industrial action, IT outages. Reefer plug capacity: 2,400 points for ~4,000 weekly reefer moves. WWG Tilbury alternative requires pre-approved BCP and additional transport.
examples.seasonality: Worse Oct-Dec (pre-Christmas surge) and during North Sea storms
examples.rootCause: Port capacity vs demand imbalance; limited berth windows; reefer plug infrastructure constraints
examples.mitigationStrategy: WWG pre-approved Tilbury as alternative; divert if congestion >3 days; use inland rail clearance at DIRFT; stagger vessel booking across 2-week windows
examples.workaroundDescription: Diversion to Tilbury (Essex) - adds 1 day transport but avoids Felixstowe queue. WWG BCP pre-approved at Tilbury. Road to Daventry 195 km / 3.5 hours.

examples.name: Cold Chain Temperature Breach Risk
examples.description: Temperature excursions during container handling transitions. Reefer offline during crane lifts (3-5 min). Tropical ports (Brisbane 30-38C, Singapore 32-35C) cause rapid warming if pre-cool insufficient or seals degraded. WWG requires dual data loggers per container.
examples.typicalDelay: No delay - but potential total loss (AUD 185,000+ per container)
examples.seasonality: Worse Dec-Feb (peak ambient temperatures at Brisbane and Singapore)
examples.rootCause: Container handling transitions; reefer power disconnection during crane ops; seal degradation; tropical ambient temperatures
examples.mitigationStrategy: WWG mandates pre-trip inspection; dual data loggers (Sensitech TempTale Ultra); pre-cool to -1C (thermal buffer); minimise Singapore dwell; carrier contractual max 3-hour unplug

examples.name: FTA Origin Proof Complexity
examples.description: AU-UK FTA preferential tariff (0% vs 12% MFN on beef). Self-declaration regime means Darling Downs bears verification burden. HMRC retrospective audits (up to 5 years) can reclaim duties + penalties from WWG as importer.
examples.typicalDelay: No delay - but retrospective duty clawback risk GBP 25,000-100,000 per audit
examples.rootCause: Self-declaration origin regime complexity; wholly-obtained vs substantial transformation rules; HMRC retrospective audit powers
examples.mitigationStrategy: Darling Downs provides full farm-to-slaughter NLIS traceability; WWG quarterly origin compliance audits; annual FTA legal review by Clearfreight

examples.cascadeEffect: Shelf-life erosion reduces WWG wholesale distribution window; potential customer order cancellation or price markdown
Shelf-life drops below 45 days; war risk surcharge absorbed by WWG; multiple containers affected simultaneously; customer delivery commitments breached

examples.regulatoryConsequence: Increased future inspection frequency if non-conformance found
examples.cascadeEffect: Demurrage accumulates; WWG cold store booking missed; wholesale customer delivery delayed; order cancellation risk
Redirected to Lineage overflow facility; higher transport cost; container detained at port GBP 85/day; customer delivery schedule disrupted

examples.regulatoryConsequence: APHA investigation; increased inspection rate 6 months; WWG import profile downgraded
examples.cascadeEffect: Total container loss; insurance claim 3-6 months; APHA increases ALL AU beef inspection rate 15% to 30%; reputational damage with WWG wholesale customers; competitor advantage window
Margin compressed from 12% to 2-4%; competitive pressure from South American suppliers; WWG wholesale customers negotiate harder

examples.statement: Chilled beef shipped Brisbane to Felixstowe via Suez (Singapore transhipment) will arrive within 35 days of dispatch 80% of the time, with 95% arriving within 40 days
examples.baselineValue: Historical average 33 days (2023-2025, n=142, Darling Downs shipments)
examples.measurementMethod: Container GPS tracking gate-out Brisbane to gate-in Felixstowe
examples.dataSource: Hapag-Lloyd container tracking API + SCT Logistics booking records 2023-2025 + WWG intake records
examples.learningNote: Red Sea instability adds 2-3 day variability even when Suez open. Hapag schedule reliability declined 72% to 65% in 2025. WWG needs to plan for 37-day median from 2026.
examples.nextIteration: Refine to 36-day P80 target for 2026; add carrier reliability weighting; model Tilbury diversion impact

examples.statement: Chilled beef will arrive at WWG Daventry cold store with minimum 50 days remaining shelf life, providing 45+ days for wholesale distribution to food service and independent retail
examples.baselineValue: 90-day total shelf life; 3 days processing + 35 days transit + 3 days BCP/haulage = 41 days consumed
examples.predictedValue: 49 days remaining at WWG Daventry DC arrival
examples.measurementMethod: Shelf-life = slaughter date + 90 days - arrival date at WWG Daventry
examples.dataSource: Darling Downs dispatch records + WWG intake records 2025-2026
examples.actualOutcome: 2025 full year: mean 49 days remaining; 68% had >50 days; 5 shipments <45 days (3 Suez-disrupted, 2 BCP-delayed)
examples.variance: 0 days vs prediction; 5 below threshold (vs 3 predicted)
examples.learningNote: Cape reroutes consume shelf-life buffer entirely. BCP physical inspections consuming more days than modelled. WWG needs 95-day product for Cape route or switch to frozen. Wholesale customers less demanding than retail (40-day minimum vs 45).
examples.nextIteration: Model separately: Suez route (49-day) vs Cape route (37-day). Wholesale channel can accept 40-day minimum. If Cape probability >20%, activate frozen conversion.

examples.statement: WWG Daventry cold storage operating costs will increase 8-12% annually through 2028 driven by energy costs, reaching GBP 34-38 per pallet-week by end 2027, making owned-DC vs outsourced cost parity by 2028
examples.baselineValue: GBP 24 per pallet-week (Jan 2026 WWG internal rate); GBP 28 Lineage overflow rate
examples.predictedValue: GBP 34 per pallet-week by Dec 2027 (internal); GBP 36 Lineage
examples.measurementMethod: Quarterly internal cost review + Lineage contract rate + UK Cold Chain Federation annual survey
examples.dataSource: WWG Daventry operational finance; UK Cold Chain Federation 2025; Lineage contract history; BEIS energy price projections
examples.learningNote: Energy is ~55% of WWG Daventry cost (slightly lower than industry 60% due to recent solar panel investment). Government support schemes expiring 2026. New builds (Northampton, Dartford) may ease capacity by 2028.

examples.statement: APHA physical inspection rate for AU beef will decrease from 15% to 5% by 2028 as Australia achieves trusted country status under evolving UK SPS framework, reducing WWG average BCP dwell by 1.5 days
examples.measurementMethod: Published APHA inspection rate data + DEFRA policy announcements + WWG BCP experience records
examples.dataSource: DEFRA BTOM review docs; APHA quarterly stats; AU-UK SPS Committee minutes
examples.learningNote: Highly dependent on UK government policy. If any AU food safety incident occurs, rate could increase for all importers including WWG. Low confidence reflects political uncertainty.

examples.statement: Brisbane reefer availability will remain below 85% utilisation Oct-Mar, with WWG Hapag volume contract reducing impact to 40% of shipments experiencing 3+ day wait (vs 60% industry average)
examples.baselineValue: 2024-25 peak: 65% of industry shipments experienced 3+ day wait; WWG rate 50% (Hapag contract benefit)
examples.predictedValue: 40% of WWG shipments affected (contract improvement + MSC fallback)
examples.measurementMethod: Days from booking confirmation to container available for pre-cool (WWG-specific tracking)
examples.dataSource: SCT booking records; Hapag equipment dashboard; WWG logistics tracking; Port of Brisbane reefer yard data
examples.actualOutcome: 2024-25 season: 50% of WWG shipments affected (better than industry 65% but worse than predicted 45%)
examples.variance: +5% - Hapag contract helped but Chinese reefer demand competing for same pool
examples.learningNote: Volume contract provides priority but not immunity. Chinese pork/seafood exports draw reefers to Asia. MSC fallback used 4 times in 2025. Consider SOC fleet for peak season.
examples.nextIteration: Factor in Chinese export volume forecasts; model SOC fleet break-even; evaluate ONE as tertiary carrier option

examples.name: Suez Canal Full Closure - Cape Reroute Required
examples.triggerCondition: Suez Canal closed to commercial shipping for >14 days
examples.assumptions: Cape routing available; no additional chokepoint conflict; carrier capacity maintained; WWG customers accept delayed delivery
examples.costChange: +AUD 12,000-18,000 per container (fuel + war risk + schedule disruption)
examples.shelfLifeImpact: Remaining drops to 35-39 days - below 40-day WWG wholesale minimum for some food service buyers
examples.mitigationPlan: WWG activates frozen conversion protocol for Cape-routed shipments; negotiate food service 35-day acceptance at 8% discount; activate Cape pricing surcharge to customers; consider airfreight for high-value fillet portions
examples.predictiveSignals: Houthi attack frequency increasing; war risk surcharge announcements; vessel rerouting visible on MarineTraffic; Hapag advisory notices

examples.name: Christmas Peak Crunch - Storage + Demand Collision
examples.triggerCondition: Nov-Dec with WWG Daventry DC >95% AND UK cold storage >97% AND Felixstowe >90% berth occupancy AND wholesale demand +20%
examples.assumptions: Normal weather; no industrial action; UK economy stable; WWG customer demand in line with seasonal norms
examples.costChange: +35% Lineage overflow storage; +15% UK haulage; +10% demurrage risk; WWG DC overtime costs +20%
examples.capacityChange: -30% available cold storage at Daventry; Lineage overflow required for 40% of volume
examples.demandImpact: +20% wholesale pull-through; food service Christmas menu pre-orders spike
examples.shelfLifeImpact: 2-4 days additional consumed by port and storage delays
examples.mitigationPlan: WWG pre-positions 4 weeks buffer by end-Oct; book Lineage overflow by Aug; Tilbury alternative pre-cleared; arrivals Tue-Thu only; customer deposit scheme for Christmas orders; temporary staff for DC
examples.predictiveSignals: WWG DC >90% by mid-Oct; Lineage >93% by mid-Oct; Felixstowe berth >85%; Hapag carrier reliability <60%

examples.name: Temperature Breach at Singapore - BCP Rejection
examples.triggerCondition: Reefer compressor fails during Singapore transhipment; product >5C for >2 hours; detected at Felixstowe BCP via data logger download
examples.timeHorizon: Per shipment (350/year = ~18 incidents over 5 years statistical expectation)
examples.assumptions: Dual data logger functional; APHA inspects temp records; breach is genuine and not logger artefact
examples.costChange: Total loss AUD 185,000 per container; insurance recovery 80%; carrier recovery Hague-Visby
examples.shelfLifeImpact: Total loss - product destroyed under APHA supervision
examples.mitigationPlan: Insurance claim (80% recovery); carrier claim under Hague-Visby; demand reefer pre-trip records from Hapag; WWG evaluates direct Brisbane-Felixstowe service (no transhipment) at +AUD 3,000/container; satellite reefer monitoring investment AUD 45,000/year
examples.predictiveSignals: Reefer unit age >8 years; Singapore ambient >34C; transhipment dwell >48 hours; marginal compressor on pre-trip inspection; no genset backup confirmed

examples.name: UK BTOM Tightening - Inspection Rates Doubled
examples.triggerCondition: UK announces Phase 3 BTOM: SPS physical checks on non-EU meat from 15% to 30%, effective Q2 2027
examples.assumptions: UK prioritises food safety; no AU SPS equivalence agreement; farming lobby pressure; applies equally to all non-EU importers including WWG
examples.costChange: +GBP 3,000-5,000 per consignment in BCP fees, demurrage, and additional shelf-life wastage
examples.shelfLifeImpact: -1-3 days consumed by inspection; more shipments falling below 40-day threshold
examples.mitigationPlan: WWG lobbies via AMIC and UK Meat Importers Association; accelerates trusted trader application; invests in pre-shipment microbiological testing (AUD 1,200/container); considers Tilbury BCP as alternative (lower volume, shorter queues); factor into customer pricing from Q3 2027
examples.predictiveSignals: DEFRA consultation papers; parliamentary food safety select committee reports; farming lobby (NFU) activity; any AU food safety incident internationally

examples.name: Shelf-Life Erosion leads to Customer Rejection / Markdown
examples.description: Any delay consuming shelf life pushes remaining days below customer thresholds. WWG wholesale customers require 40+ days at intake (food service) or 45+ days (independent retail). Below 35: unsaleable, diverted to pet food/rendering at 60% loss. Wholesale channel more forgiving than supermarket retail but still time-critical.
examples.triggerDelay: Immediate - every day of delay erodes shelf life by one day
examples.affectedParties: WWG (bears rejection cost and customer relationship damage); exporter (reputational, future order risk); wholesale customers (menu planning disruption)
examples.preventionAction: Maintain minimum 52-day shelf life at dispatch; switch to frozen for >40 day transit routes; WWG to negotiate 35-day food service acceptance for event-driven demand

examples.name: Demand-Led Cost Escalation leads to Margin Compression
examples.description: Multiple cost increases compound: freight +15%, storage +35%, demurrage GBP 85/day, haulage +25%, insurance +10%. Total landed cost +20-40% during peaks. WWG margin compressed from target 14% to 3-5%. Cannot fully pass through to wholesale customers (quarterly price lock contracts).
examples.triggerDelay: 1-4 weeks (cost increases phase in across chain)
examples.affectedParties: WWG (margin compression, cash flow pressure); exporter (higher CIF base requested); wholesale customers (eventual price increases at quarterly review)
examples.preventionAction: Annual freight rates with Hapag; wholesale customer cost-escalation clauses from 2027 contracts; AUD/GBP forward hedging 50% of exposure; pre-buy buffer stock at off-peak rates

examples.name: Cold Storage Overflow leads to Container Detention at Port
examples.description: When WWG Daventry DC full AND Lineage overflow at capacity, containers cannot be stripped. Sit at Felixstowe or on chassis. Detention GBP 85/day starts day 5. Multiple containers backing up takes 2-3 weeks to clear. Felixstowe reefer plug scarcity means containers may lose power.
examples.triggerDelay: 3-5 days after cold store and overflow reach capacity
examples.affectedParties: WWG (detention costs, product risk); port (yard congestion); shipping line (equipment off-hire); Lineage (SLA breach)
examples.preventionAction: Secondary cold store at Grimsby pre-arranged; stagger arrivals across 3-week windows; pre-Christmas stock build starts October; WWG DC expansion business case in progress for 2027

examples.name: Temperature Breach leads to Industry-Wide Inspection Increase
examples.description: ONE bad container triggers APHA to increase inspection rate for ALL Australian beef imports (not just offending exporter/importer). Rate increases 15% to 30% for 6+ months. Most damaging unintended consequence: one exporter's failure affects entire AU industry and every UK importer including WWG. WWG suffers increased BCP delays even if their containers are compliant.
examples.affectedParties: ALL AU beef exporters to UK; ALL UK importers of AU beef including WWG; APHA (doubled workload); BCP operators (capacity strain)
examples.preventionAction: WWG mandates zero-tolerance pre-trip inspection via Hapag contract; dual data loggers; avoid Singapore transhipment in peak summer Dec-Feb; industry reefer quality programme via AMIC; WWG evaluates direct service option

examples.name: Claims History leads to Insurance Premium Increase
examples.description: Marine cargo premiums increase 15-25% following total-loss claim. War risk adds 0.05-0.15% of cargo value. After 2+ claims in 12 months, QBE may impose deductible increases or decline renewal. AUD 10,000-18,000 additional annually for WWG corridor.
examples.affectedParties: WWG (premium payer as CIF importer); exporter (origin cargo insurance); QBE/insurer (claims ratio deterioration)
examples.timeToRecover: 2-3 claims-free years to restore base rate
examples.preventionAction: WWG risk management programme documented for QBE; loss prevention investment (satellite monitoring, dual loggers); multi-insurer panel (QBE + Allianz); contractual carrier responsibility

examples.name: Repeated Delays leads to Wholesale Customer Switches to Competitor
examples.description: WWG wholesale customers (food service, hospitality groups, independent retail chains) operate on reliability KPIs: OTIF >92%, shelf-life compliance >95%. If AU beef consistently fails (3+ occasions in 6 months), buyer diversifies to Irish or South American beef via competing wholesaler. Once lost, WWG re-listing takes 6-12 months (shorter cycle than retail due to less formal procurement).
examples.triggerDelay: 3-6 months (customer tolerance then supplier review and switch)
examples.affectedParties: WWG (lost revenue, stranded DC capacity, customer acquisition cost); exporter (reduced UK volume); wholesale customers (transition disruption, potential quality change)
examples.timeToRecover: 6-12 months (re-listing cycle via WWG commercial team)
examples.preventionAction: WWG dedicated account manager per key customer; proactive delay notification 48 hours before impact; UK buffer stock 2-week minimum at Daventry; dual-corridor strategy (Brisbane + Melbourne) for resilience; customer loyalty pricing for annual commitments

examples.name: Annual Reefer Crunch Cycle (Brisbane Oct-Mar)
examples.description: Every year Brisbane reefer availability drops sharply Oct-Mar as AU meat/dairy/fruit export season peaks. Troughs Dec-Jan. Recovers by April. WWG Hapag contract provides partial protection but not immunity.
examples.leadIndicators: Hapag equipment dashboard >80% utilisation by September; Port reefer yard occupancy reports; carrier surcharge announcements; Chinese export volume forecasts
examples.typicalImpact: 3-14 day equipment wait; 15-25% of industry shipments require alternative port/carrier; WWG impact 10-15% (contract protection); AUD 2,000-5,000 premium per container
examples.recommendedAction: WWG to book reefer equipment 8 weeks ahead for Oct-Mar via Hapag contract; activate MSC as secondary carrier by September; evaluate SOC fleet for peak season 2027

examples.name: North Sea Storm Delays (Felixstowe Oct-Feb)
examples.description: North Sea storms cause 1-4 day vessel delays, 3-5 events per winter. Port closure (wind >40 knots) causes vessel bunching and 3-7 day post-storm congestion. WWG Tilbury alternative available but adds transport complexity.
examples.leadIndicators: Met Office severe weather warnings; Felixstowe operational status feed; vessel schedule delays >24 hours; North Sea wave height forecasts
examples.typicalImpact: 1-4 day vessel delay; 3-7 day post-storm congestion; demurrage GBP 85/day; missed BCP slots; Tilbury diversion adds 1 day + GBP 800 transport
examples.recommendedAction: WWG monitors Met Office 5-day forecast; pre-book BCP with 2-day buffer window; Tilbury as pre-approved storm-alternative; inform wholesale customers proactively

examples.name: UK BBQ Season Demand Surge (May-Aug)
examples.description: UK beef demand +25-35% during BBQ season. Steak cuts highest uplift. WWG wholesale customers (pubs, restaurants, event caterers) order 4-8 weeks ahead. Late surge (unexpected heatwave) causes 40-60% spot premium. WWG food service customers less forward-planned than retail.
examples.leadIndicators: Met Office seasonal outlook; WWG customer forward order volumes Mar-Apr; pub/restaurant booking data; major event calendar (festivals, sporting events)
examples.typicalImpact: Volume +25-35%; price premium 10-20% contract, 40-60% spot; cold storage >95%; haulage surcharges; WWG DC overtime
examples.recommendedAction: WWG secures forward contracts with Darling Downs by Feb; pre-builds 2-week buffer by end-Apr; locks storage and transport by Mar; customer deposit scheme for guaranteed BBQ supply

examples.name: AUD/GBP Exchange Rate Commodity Cycle
examples.description: AUD/GBP correlates with commodity prices (iron ore, coal, LNG). AUD strong = AU beef more expensive in GBP = WWG margin compressed. AUD weak = competitive = demand rises but exporter may seek higher AUD prices. 18-24 month cycle. WWG hedges 50% of quarterly exposure.
examples.leadIndicators: Iron ore spot price trend; RBA policy statements; China PMI/steel demand; commodity futures curve; Bank of England rate decisions
examples.typicalImpact: 5-12% landed cost variation; demand elasticity ~0.6; margin impact GBP 8,000-25,000 per container at extremes; WWG hedging reduces to 3-6%
examples.recommendedAction: WWG forward hedges 50% at budget rate (currently AUD 0.52/GBP); maintains GBP revenue account; quarterly wholesale customer price adjustment linked to FX band; Darling Downs contract includes AUD/GBP corridor clause

examples._section: === INCIDENT EXAMPLES WITH ESCALATION CHAINS (3 Incidents) ===

examples.description: Container HLCU-7294618 (WWG consignment WWG-2025-DDB-034) reefer compressor failed during Singapore transhipment. Temperature rose from 0.5C to 7.2C over 4 hours. Dual data loggers (Sensitech TempTale Ultra) both recorded +5C excursion for 2h 40m. Detected at Felixstowe BCP during mandatory data logger download. Product: 24 tonnes sirloin/ribeye primals valued AUD 185,000 CIF.
examples.rootCause: Reefer compressor unit age 9 years; Singapore yard unplugged container 6 hours during peak congestion; genset not attached during cranage contrary to Hapag contractual obligation
examples.resolution: Container rejected by APHA Official Veterinarian. Product destroyed under supervision at Felixstowe. Insurance claim AUD 148,000 (80% recovery from QBE). Carrier claim AUD 12,000 under Hague-Visby. Hapag committed max 3-hour unplug policy and mandatory genset for all WWG reefers at Singapore. WWG added pre-transhipment temperature check to SOP.
examples.timeToDetect: 28 days (not detected until BCP data logger download at Felixstowe)
examples.timeToResolve: 4 months (insurance settlement 3 months + carrier settlement 4 months)
examples.lessonLearned: WWG investing in real-time satellite reefer monitoring (Orbcomm) AUD 45,000/year. Demand genset backup in all carrier contracts. Evaluate direct Brisbane-Felixstowe service (ONE, Evergreen) for high-value shipments. Dual logger policy validated - both loggers agreed within 0.3C.

examples.description: IPAFFS pre-notification for HLCU-8831227 (WWG consignment WWG-2026-DDB-003) rejected by system: commodity code 0201.30.00 (chilled boneless beef) entered but DAFF health certificate specified 0202.30.90 (frozen boneless beef). Root cause: Darling Downs originally booked as frozen then switched to chilled at WWG request without updating health cert commodity code.
examples.rootCause: Manual data entry across disconnected systems; health cert for original frozen booking not updated when WWG changed to chilled; no pre-submission cross-check between IPAFFS, CDS, and health cert commodity codes
examples.resolution: DAFF issued amended health cert under 24-hour emergency procedure (AUD 450 fee). IPAFFS resubmitted by WWG compliance team. Container held 2 days at Felixstowe. Demurrage GBP 170 (2 days). Total cost GBP 620. WWG implemented mandatory pre-submission 3-way cross-check (health cert vs IPAFFS vs CDS commodity codes).
examples.lessonLearned: WWG now requires mandatory pre-submission cross-check: health cert commodity code vs IPAFFS commodity code vs commercial invoice line items vs CDS tariff heading. Added to WWG Import SOP v3.2. Evaluating IPAFFS API auto-populate tool (estimated AUD 15,000 development cost, 6-month payback).

examples.description: Storm Eowyn closed Felixstowe for 36 hours (wind gusts 55 knots). 12 vessels queued on approach. Post-storm 4-day congestion as bunched vessels competed for berth windows. Reefer plug availability dropped to 60%. Three WWG containers (WWG-2025-DDB-041, 042, 043) waited 5 days. Christmas peak demand compounded everything - Daventry DC at 97% when containers finally released.
examples.rootCause: North Sea storm (force majeure); vessel bunching post-closure; limited reefer plug capacity at 60% during recovery; Christmas peak staffing; Daventry DC near capacity
examples.resolution: Container -041 diverted to Tilbury via WWG pre-approved alternative (arrived 1 day late, BCP cleared same day). Containers -042 and -043 waited 5 days at Felixstowe. -042 delivered to food service customer 3 days late (accepted with 42 days shelf life remaining). -043 had 38 days remaining - WWG applied 12% markdown for fast-turn food service customer. Total cost: GBP 16,200 (demurrage GBP 2,550 + Tilbury transport GBP 800 + markdown GBP 8,400 + overtime/expediting GBP 4,450).
examples.timeToDetect: Immediate (Met Office warning + Felixstowe closure announcement)
examples.timeToResolve: 7 days (all three containers stripped and distributed)
examples.lessonLearned: WWG pre-Christmas arrivals now target Dec 10th maximum (10-day buffer before Christmas). Tilbury pre-approval validated and worked well. Need real-time port congestion dashboard integrated with WWG logistics system. Consider splitting December volume: 60% Felixstowe / 40% Tilbury during peak.

examples.triggerCondition: Temperature breach detected at BCP - product >5C for >1 hour on either data logger
examples.responsibleParty: WWG Trade Compliance Manager + Darling Downs QA Manager
examples.prescribedActions: Quarantine container at BCP, notify QBE insurer, initiate Hapag carrier claim, alert APHA of data logger evidence, photograph product condition, notify WWG MD
examples.decisionAuthority: Accept/reject product; authorise destruction; file insurance claim up to AUD 200,000; suspend future shipments from same reefer unit

examples.triggerCondition: Total loss confirmed; insurance claim >AUD 100,000; carrier liability dispute; or APHA increases inspection rate
examples.responsibleParty: WWG Managing Director + Darling Downs Commercial Director
examples.prescribedActions: Formal QBE insurance claim submission, Hapag carrier liability letter (legal), AMIC notification, root cause investigation report, corrective action plan to APHA, review Hapag contract terms, evaluate direct service option, satellite monitoring investment decision
examples.decisionAuthority: Authorise legal action; negotiate settlement; approve supply chain design changes; commit capital expenditure for monitoring systems; review carrier relationship

examples.prescribedActions: Identify IPAFFS rejection reason, contact Darling Downs/DAFF if amended health cert needed, correct commodity codes, resubmit IPAFFS, notify BCP of delay, inform Clearfreight UK for CDS alignment
examples.decisionAuthority: Resubmit IPAFFS; request amended health cert from DAFF; authorise demurrage up to GBP 500; delay BCP appointment

examples.triggerCondition: Port congestion >3 days AND shelf life at risk on 2+ WWG containers
examples.responsibleParty: WWG Supply Chain Manager + SCT Logistics Account Manager
examples.prescribedActions: Assess Tilbury diversion feasibility, contact Tilbury BCP for slot, arrange Reed Boardall or alternative haulage from Tilbury, negotiate emergency Lineage overflow if Daventry full, notify affected wholesale customers, calculate shelf-life per container and triage by remaining days
examples.decisionAuthority: Authorise Tilbury diversion (GBP 2,000-5,000); emergency haulage booking; negotiate customer markdown vs rejection; authorise Lineage overflow at premium rate

examples.commodity: Chilled grass-fed beef - sirloin, ribeye, fillet, rump (vacuum-packed primals and subprimals)
